meta.title: Trek & Wars — Comics + Books Map
meta.note: Full publishing history of Star Trek & Star Wars comics and novels, 1967–present. Self-contained; this list is the single source of truth — add a series by adding one entry. Goes stale over time by design.

entries.name: Star Trek (Gold Key)
entries.desc: The very first Star Trek comics. 61 issues across 12 years, published while the original series aired and in syndication. Gold Key artists had never seen the show — they worked from publicity photos, which is why Spock sometimes looks wrong. Beloved for their wild, unconstrained stories. Pre-dates IDW's licence by decades.

entries.name: Star Trek (Marvel 1979–1982)
entries.desc: Marvel's first crack at Trek, tying into The Motion Picture. 18 issues. Set in the movie era with the refit Enterprise. Ended when the licence lapsed. Often overlooked but includes some genuinely solid stories.

entries.name: Star Trek (DC 1984–1996)
entries.desc: DC held the licence across two volumes (1984–1988, 1989–1996). Set during and after the film era, covering original stories with the TOS crew and later TNG. Some of the most acclaimed pre-IDW Trek comics. 80+ issues across both runs.

entries.name: Star Trek (WildStorm/Tokyopop)
entries.desc: WildStorm (DC imprint) held a short licence in the late 90s/early 2000s covering TNG, DS9, and Voyager eras. Tokyopop produced manga-style Trek in the mid-2000s. Niche period between the DC and IDW eras.

entries.name: Star Trek: Countdown + Kelvin Ongoing
entries.desc: IDW's Kelvin Timeline era — the 2009 Countdown prequel (written by the film's screenwriters) plus a 60-issue ongoing retelling TOS stories with Pine/Quinto crew. Essential companion to the Abrams films.

entries.name: IDW TV Era (Discovery/Picard/SNW)
entries.desc: A run of miniseries tying into each new streaming era: Discovery (Succession, Aftermath), Picard (Countdown, Stargazer), and Strange New Worlds (Illyrian Enigma, Scorpius Run). Omnibus collections available for each.

entries.name: IDW Shared Universe (Sisko / Defiant era)
entries.desc: The most ambitious IDW Trek run: interconnected flagship (ST2022, Sisko/USS Theseus) + Defiant spinoff (Worf's rogue crew) + Sons of Star Trek + crossover events Day of Blood and Lore War. 58 issues total across the two main series. The gold standard of modern Trek comics.

entries.name: IDW Current (Red Shirts / SNW / Voyager)
entries.desc: Three concurrent miniseries: Red Shirts (TOS-era security officers), SNW: Seeds of Destruction (Strange New Worlds S4 tie-in), and Voyager: Homecoming (post-Delta Quadrant). Plus the SFA: Lost Contact tie-in. Bridging to the 2026 relaunch.

entries.name: IDW 2026 Relaunch (Seven / Raffi)
entries.desc: New ongoing flagship (Captain Seven / Enterprise-G, writer Cantwell) plus Zero Point (Captain Raffi, writer Charlie Jane Anders) both launching September–October 2026. Set post-Picard S3. The next era of IDW Trek.

entries.name: James Blish Adaptations
entries.desc: The first Star Trek books ever. SF author James Blish adapted all 79 TOS episodes into short story collections across 12 volumes (1967–1977). Working from scripts, not footage, they're often subtly different from the aired episodes. Historical curiosities and fan essentials. Blish also wrote the first original Trek novel, Spock Must Die! (1970).

entries.name: Bantam Original Novels
entries.desc: 13 original novels published by Bantam alongside the Blish adaptations. Includes early fan-favourite stories like The Price of the Phoenix (1977). These predate Pocket Books and exist in their own continuity bubble. Largely out of print — hunt them in used bookshops.

entries.name: Pocket Books Numbered Series
entries.desc: The backbone of Star Trek novel publishing. Pocket Books took the licence in 1979 and ran numbered TOS novels from #1 through to the 90s. Early standouts include The Entropy Effect (1981, Vonda McIntyre) and The Wrath of Khan novelisation. Expanded to cover TNG, DS9, and Voyager with separate numbered lines throughout the 1990s.

entries.name: Pocket Books Litverse
entries.desc: The most ambitious Star Trek literary project ever attempted. Pocket Books built a shared-universe continuation of DS9, TNG, and Voyager post-series, told across 150+ interconnected novels over 20 years. Authors include David Mack, Una McCormack, Christopher L. Bennett. The "Destiny" trilogy (Mack, 2008) is widely considered the peak. Ended when Paramount locked down the licence for the new streaming era.

entries.name: Gallery Books / Modern Tie-ins
entries.desc: Simon & Schuster rebranded to Gallery Books in 2019. Now publishes official tie-in novels for Discovery, Picard, Strange New Worlds, and Starfleet Academy — all aligned with the streaming canon. More accessible entry points than the Litverse, but shallower universe-building.

entries.name: Star Wars (Marvel 1977–1986)
entries.desc: The original Star Wars comics. Marvel launched their Star Wars series in 1977 as a film adaptation, then ran 107 issues of original stories. Because they'd committed before the film proved itself, they essentially co-created the SW expanded universe. Includes the first appearance of Jaxxon (the green rabbit). Legends continuity. Full run on Marvel Unlimited. Omnibus collections available.

entries.name: Dark Horse Classic Era
entries.desc: The golden age of Star Wars comics. Dark Horse held the licence for 24 years and produced over 900 issues across dozens of series. Key titles: Dark Empire (1991, Luke falls to the dark side), Tales of the Jedi (ancient Sith wars), X-Wing: Rogue Squadron, Knights of the Old Republic (4000 BBY), Republic (Clone Wars), Dark Times (post-Order 66), and Legacy (130 ABY). All Legends continuity. The definitive pre-Disney SW comics.

entries.name: Marvel Relaunch — SW + Vader + Aphra
entries.desc: Marvel's return to Star Wars after Disney's acquisition. 290+ issues across multiple simultaneous ongoings: SW flagship (Jason Aaron/Gillen/Pak), Darth Vader (Gillen/Pak — introduces Aphra), Doctor Aphra (Wong), Bounty Hunters, plus major crossover events War of the Bounty Hunters, Crimson Reign, Hidden Empire, and Dark Droids. All on Marvel Unlimited. The Kieron Gillen Vader run and Greg Pak Vader 2020 are must-reads.

entries.name: High Republic Comics (Marvel + Dark Horse)
entries.desc: Both Marvel (adult-focused, Cavan Scott) and Dark Horse (YA-focused, Daniel José Older) ran simultaneous High Republic comics across three phases. Set 200–400 years before the films. The Nihil threat, ancient Jedi civilisation. Unusually, two publishers held simultaneous licences for different tonal targets within the same era — Marvel for main line, Dark Horse as the YA imprint. This is why both exist at the same time.

entries.name: Marvel Current (2025 Relaunch)
entries.desc: Current Marvel Star Wars: the 2025 flagship (New Republic era, Alex Segura/Phil Noto), Legacy of Vader, and Shadow of Maul miniseries. Post-ROTJ era. Clean jumping-on point. All on Marvel Unlimited.

entries.name: Dark Horse Current (Bad Batch / Pathfinders)
entries.desc: Dark Horse's current active titles: The Bad Batch: Rogue Agents (Clone Force 99 post-Clone Wars) and High Republic Adventures: Pathfinders (new HR chapter from George Mann). Both day-and-date on GlobalComix.

entries.name: ANH Novelisation + Splinter
entries.desc: The very first Star Wars media. The ANH novelisation (credited to George Lucas, ghostwritten by Alan Dean Foster) was published in November 1976 — a year before the film. Splinter of the Mind's Eye (Foster, 1978) was written as a potential low-budget sequel if ANH flopped. It's set on Mimban and features Luke vs Vader — canonically awkward post-ESB but historically fascinating.

entries.name: Han Solo / Lando Adventures
entries.desc: Brian Daley's Han Solo trilogy (1979–80) and L. Neil Smith's Lando Calrissian trilogy (1983). The first original SW novels after Splinter. Daley's Han Solo books are genuinely fun heist-style adventures set before ANH. Out of print but findable used. Legends continuity.

entries.name: Thrawn Trilogy (Heir to the Empire)
entries.desc: The novel that relaunched Star Wars. Timothy Zahn's Heir to the Empire (1991), Dark Force Rising, and The Last Command created Grand Admiral Thrawn and Mara Jade, and effectively invented the modern SW expanded universe. These became the blueprint for what the sequel trilogy could have been. Now Legends, but Thrawn himself was brought into canon via Rebels and Ahsoka.

entries.name: Bantam Expanded Universe Era
entries.desc: The wild west of SW novels. Bantam produced ~40 novels covering the New Republic era — everything from Kevin J. Anderson's Jedi Academy trilogy to the Courtship of Princess Leia. Quality varies wildly. The X-Wing novels (Michael Stackpole, Aaron Allston) are highlights. All Legends continuity.

entries.name: New Jedi Order + Legacy of the Force
entries.desc: Del Rey took over and went darker: the New Jedi Order (19 books, the Yuuzhan Vong invasion — major character deaths) and Legacy of the Force (9 books, Jacen Solo's fall to the dark side). Ambitious multi-author long-form storytelling. Significant investment to read in full. All Legends.

entries.name: Fate of the Jedi + Last Books of Legends
entries.desc: Fate of the Jedi (9 books, Luke exiled) and various standalone novels up to Disney's April 2014 Legends declaration. The Legends universe's final chapter. Ended mid-story when the canon reset came.

entries.name: Canon Novel Era
entries.desc: Post-2014 Disney canon novels. Spans all eras from High Republic to post-sequel trilogy. Highlights: Claudia Gray's Lost Stars (widely considered the best SW novel ever), Bloodline (Leia pre-TFA), Timothy Zahn's new Thrawn canon trilogy, and the entire High Republic novel initiative. Accessible — each stands alone or in small series.

entries.name: High Republic Novels
entries.desc: The High Republic novel initiative ran across three phases (2021–2024). Adult novels by Cavan Scott, Claudia Gray, Daniel José Older, and Charles Soule. Set 200–400 years before the films in the height of the Jedi Order. Phase I's Light of the Jedi (Scott) is an excellent entry point.
